elements.sent-2411-001: We examine the higher-spin gauge models which are free on-shell and whose cubic vertex is BRST-exact.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show that these are free off-shell as well as on-shell.
elements.sent-2411-003: The key equation for this relates the derivative of the total deformed action $S(g)$ with respect to the deformation parameter $g$ to an $S(g)$-exact term.
elements.sent-2411-004: In the realm of string theory, they are expected to reveal characteristic features in the high-energy limit\cite{Gross (see also \cite{Sagnotti tensionless limit ).}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Free higher-spin gauge theories are now well understood, while interacting theories have not been understood well even for massless higher-spin gauge fields in flat spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-006: There are several obstacles to construct intractions, one of which is the no-go theorem\cite{no-go}\footnote{ See \cite{KU81 , for an analysis in the field theory framework.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: To avoid this, the spin value and the number of the derivatives contained in vertices should be restricted so that higher-spin gauge fields are not included in the asymptotic states.
elements.sent-2411-008: By applying the BRST deformation scheme\cite{BH9304 in the BRST-antifield formalism, higher-spin gauge models of massless totally-symmetric tensors are constructed in \cite{SS2011}\footnote{ See \cite{BDGH0007, BRST cohomology,HLGR1206,HLGR1310, Rahman1905 for other attempts by using BRST cohomological metod.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Subsequently, these are generalized to include massless totally-symmetric tensor-spinors in \cite{FKSS2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: For example, the higher-spin gauge model of two bosons $\{\phi,\ \phi_1\}$ is given as \begin{equation}\label{def. action}S(g)\ =\ S^0+\sum^\infty_{n=1}g^nS^n,\end{equation} where $g$ is the deformation parameter.
elements.sent-2411-011: Furthermore, it is shown that the cubic vertex is BRST-exact \begin{equation}S^1\ =\delta_BR^1\ =\ (R^1,S^0),\qquad R^1=-\frac{1}{2}\int\ d^Dx\ \tr\Big[ \phi_1^T\tilde{G}(\phi\phi^*_1) +(\tilde\partial c\phi_1^*)^T\phi^*_1 \Big]. \label{eqn:R1}\end{equation} However, whether the deformed total action $S(g)$ is free off-shell or not was left as an interesting future problem.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this letter, we tackle this issue and show that this higher-spin gauge model reduces to free off-shell as well.
elements.sent-2411-013: For our purpose, we show that there exists $\mathfrak{R (g)$ satisfying the differential equation \begin{equation}\label{diff. eq.}\frac{d}{dg}S(g)\ =\ \Big(S(g),R(g)\Big),\end{equation} which means that the derivative of $S(g)$ with respect to $g$ is $S$-exact.}
elements.sent-2411-014: This is the key relation to relate $S(g)$ to $S^0$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Expanding $\mathfrak{R (g)$ in terms of $g$ as \begin{equation}R(g)\ =\ \sum^\infty_{n=1}g^{n-1}R^n,\end{equation} the differential equation (\ref{diff.}}

elements.sent-2411-020: Now we are ready to show that $S(g)$ reduces to $S^0$.
elements.sent-2411-021: By an infinitesimal change of $g$: $g\mapsto g+\delta g$, the deformed action $S(g)$ becomes \begin{equation}\label{infinitesimal tr.} S(g+\delta g)\ \simeq \ S(g)+\delta g\frac{d}{dg}S(g).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-022: Since the second term in the right hand side is $S$-exact thanks to the differential equation (\ref{diff.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Eq. ), it can be eliminated by field redefinitions.
elements.sent-2411-024: In fact, the field redefinition $\Phi^A\mapsto \Phi^A+\delta gu^A$ and $\Phi^*_A\mapsto \Phi^*_A+\delta gv_A$ causes the change $S[\Phi^A,\Phi^*_A]\mapsto S[\Phi^A+gu^A,\Phi^*_A+gv_A]=S[\Phi^A,\Phi^*_A]+\delta g\Big(u^A\frac{\delta S {\delta\Phi^A +v_A\frac{\delta S {\delta\Phi^*_A}\Big)$, which can absorb $\frac{d {dg S(g)$ by choosing $u^A$ and $v_A$ appropriately.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: As a result, we obtain \begin{equation}S(g+\delta g)\ =\ S(g),\end{equation} by field redefinitions.
elements.sent-2411-026: This implies that the deformed action $S(g)$ is independent of $g$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Therefore, we may conclude that \begin{equation}S(g)\ =\ S(0)\ =\ S^0.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-030: Other models in \cite{SS2011,FKSS2024 may satisfy the similar relation and also be shown to be free off-shell by the similar argument.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Finally, we will comment on how $S^1$ becomes exact.
elements.sent-2411-032: The cubic vertex $S^1$ in \cite{FKSS2024,SS2011 is given as $S^1=\alpha^1_0+\alpha^1_1+\alpha^1_2$ where the antighost number of $\alpha_i^1$ is $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The master equation $(S(g),S(g))=0$ implies that $\alpha^1_i$ must solve the relations \begin{equation}\Gamma \alpha^1_2\ =\ 0,\qquad \Delta \alpha^1_2+\Gamma \alpha^1_1\ =\ 0,\qquad \Delta \alpha^1_1+\Gamma \alpha^1_0\ =\ 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-034: The $\Delta$ and $\Gamma$ denote the BRST transformation that change the antighost number by $-1$ and $0$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-035: On the other hand, $S^1$ should not be BRST-exact.
elements.sent-2411-036: When $S^1$ is BRST-exact, $\alpha^1_i$ may be expressed as \begin{equation}\alpha^1_2\ =\ \Gamma r_2,\qquad \alpha^1_1\ =\ \Delta r_2 +\Gamma r_1,\qquad \alpha^1_0\ =\ \Delta r_1+\Gamma r_0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: In constructing higher-spin gauge models, $\alpha_2^1=0$ was imposed, but this does not mean $r_2=0$.
elements.sent-2411-038: In fact, there may exist a non-trivial $r_2$ which satisfys $\Gamma r_1=0$ and $\Delta r_2\neq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-039: The cubic vertex $S^1$ in \bref{eqn:R1 yields such an example.}
elements.sent-2411-040: It is interesting to to consider the other cases, including non-cubic vertex $S^1$, including massive tensor and tensor-spinor, including mixed-symmetry (or, totally-antisymmetry) tensor and tensor-spinor, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-041: There may be a possibility to construct non-trivial interactions in these cases.

elements.sent-2411-044: As an example, we consider the higher-spin gauge model of two bosons, a totally-symmetric rank-$n_1$ tensor $\phi^{\mu_1\cdots\mu_{n_1 _1$ and a rank-$n$ tensor $\phi^{\mu_1\cdots\mu_n}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: We also introduce Grassmann-odd ghost fields, a rank-$(n_1-1)$ tensor $c_1^{\mu_2\cdots\mu_{n_1 }$ and a rank-$(n-1)$ tensor $c^{\mu_2\cdots\mu_n}$, and antifields $\{\phi^*_1, \phi^*, c_1^*, c^*\}$ which have the same algebraic symmetries but opposite Grassmann parity.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The action is given as \begin{equation}\label{def. action}S(g)\ =\ S^0+\sum^\infty_{n=1}g^nS^n,\end{equation} where $g$ is the deformation parameter.
elements.sent-2411-047: For an arbitrary totally-symmetric rank-$n$ tensor $A$, we have defined $\tilde{G (A)$ as $\tilde{G (A)\equiv G(A)+\frac{1 {2}\eta\partial\partial A''$ and $G(A)$ as $G(A)\equiv F(A)-\frac{1 {2}\eta F'(A)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: A prime on a tensor represents the trace, namely $A'_{\mu_3\cdots \mu_n =A_{\mu_3\cdots \mu_n { ^\rho{ _\rho$\,.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The divergence of $A$ is expressed as $\pa\cdot A_{\mu_2\cdots\mu_n =\pa^{\mu_1 A_{\mu_1\mu_2\cdots\mu_n}$\,.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: We require that the ghost $c$ is traceless for $n\geq3$, so that $F(\phi)$ is invariant under the BRST transformation $\delta_B\phi=\partial c$.

elements.sent-2411-056: Furthermore, it is shown that the cubic vertex is BRST-exact \begin{equation}S^1\ =\delta_BR^1\ =\ (R^1,S^0),\qquad R^1=-\frac{1}{2}\int\ d^Dx\ \tr\Big[ \phi_1^T\tilde{G}(\phi\phi^*_1) +(\tilde\partial c\phi_1^*)^T\phi^*_1 \Big], \label{eqn:R1}\end{equation} where the antibracket $(X,Y)$ for two functionals, $X[\Phi^A,\Phi^*_A]$ and $Y[\Phi^A,\Phi^*_A]$, is defined by \begin{equation}(X,Y)\equiv X\frac{\overleftarrow{\delta}}{\delta\Phi^A}\frac{\overrightarrow{\delta}}{\delta\Phi^*_A}Y +X\frac{\overleftarrow{\delta}}{\delta\Phi^*_A}\frac{\overrightarrow{\delta}}{\delta\Phi^A}Y,\end{equation} where $\Phi^*_A$ are the antifields corresponding to $\Phi^A=\{\phi_1, \phi, c_1, c\}$.
